meta.title: G350d Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Mercedes G350d OM656 2.9 diesel — real UK costs, DPF & fuel system insight, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

itemListElement.name: OM656 D29 R SCR (2.9L Straight-Six Twin-Turbo Diesel)

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes G350d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM656 2.9-litre diesel engine typically costs £5,500–£8,500 supply only. Used engines are available from £3,500–£5,500. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engine replacements typically around £3,500 or more. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines can exceed £14,000.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes G350d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM656 is generally considered a reliable engine — a modern, efficient straight-six diesel that delivers exceptional performance. However, it has known issues: DPF/AdBlue system faults (especially with city driving), Bosch CP4 fuel system sensitivity (poor fuel damages piezo injectors), turbocharger issues, Camtronic valve timing problems (camshaft rocker wear), and a wiring harness recall. Regular maintenance and quality fuel are essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes G350d?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are DPF clogging and AdBlue system faults (persistent pressure sensor errors, especially with city driving), fuel system issues (Bosch CP4 injectors sensitive to fuel quality), turbocharger faults (oil deposits, actuator issues, boost lag), Camtronic camshaft rocker wear (popping intake noise), and wiring harness chafing (recall for potential damage to ABS/ESP systems).

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes G350d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £80,000 G350d, a £7,000 recon is 9% — definitely worth it. Against a high-mileage £35,000 example, the same recon approaches our 70% Mercedes threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes G350d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The G350d carries the engine code OM656 D29 R SCR. It's a 2.9-litre straight-six twin-turbo diesel engine used in the G-Class W463 from 2019 onwards. The badge on the tailgate is the easiest tell — 'G 350 d'.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the DPF issues on the G350d, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: DPF issues are usually repairable without engine replacement. However, owners report persistent DPF pressure sensor failures even after replacing the DPF and sensors. If you experience recurring DPF issues, ensure you're using the correct low-ash oil, taking regular motorway runs for regeneration, and having the vehicle properly diagnosed with STAR diagnostic equipment. In some cases, the underlying cause may be EGR or wiring faults rather than the DPF itself. If DPF issues are ignored, they can lead to more serious engine damage.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the OM656 2.9-litre straight-six diesel engine. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engine replacements typically around £3,500 or more. Used OM656 engines are available from approximately £3,500–£5,500.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a G350d engine typically takes 16–22 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The G-Class's ladder-frame chassis and tight engine bay make this a complex job.

problems.title: DPF Clogging & Emissions System Faults
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light, DPF pressure sensor errors, loss of power, sluggish response, failed regeneration cycles, AdBlue system warnings.
problems.repairCost: £500–£2,000 (DPF clean, sensor replacement, or AdBlue system repair)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,500 (if emissions system damage leads to engine issues)
problems.recommendation: The G350d's DPF and AdBlue systems are sensitive to short urban journeys. Owners report persistent DPF pressure sensor failures even after replacing the DPF and sensors. One owner experienced the check engine light recurring with the same DPF pressure sensor failure after multiple replacements and forced regenerations. If you predominantly drive in town, ensure the vehicle completes regular regeneration cycles — a motorway run at 2,000+ rpm for 20 minutes at least once a week is recommended. Using the correct low-ash oil is critical for DPF longevity.

failureLink.label: Read the full DPF failure guide

problems.title: Fuel System Sensitivity — Bosch CP4 Injector Issues
problems.symptoms: Rough running, misfires, poor acceleration, engine warning light, increased fuel consumption.
problems.repairCost: £600–£2,000 (injector replacement — up to six piezo injectors)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,500 (if fuel system damage leads to engine failure)
problems.recommendation: The OM656 uses a Bosch CP4 common-rail system with sensitive piezo injectors. It does not tolerate poor-quality diesel or contamination. Even minor impurities in the fuel tank can damage the injectors, while a clogged fuel filter disrupts combustion and leads to power loss. Regular fuel filter changes and the use of high-quality diesel can significantly extend engine life.

failureLink.label: Read the full fuel injector failure guide

problems.title: Turbocharger & Camtronic Valve Timing Issues
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, unusual noises from the engine bay (whistling, grinding), turbo lag, popping noise from the intake manifold, engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,500 (turbocharger repair; Camtronic rocker/actuator replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,500 (if left untreated leads to engine damage)
problems.recommendation: The OM656's BorgWarner R2S turbocharger is a sensitive component. Oil deposits, worn bearings, or a contaminated charge air system can lead to power loss and unusual noises. A known turbo actuator issue involves a hose that gets too hot and collapses, causing boost faults. Cam rocker wear is also a known issue on the OM656 — it will show up as a popping sound in the intake. If you notice unusual noises or loss of power, have the turbocharger and Camtronic system inspected immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbocharger failure guide

buyingChecks.title: Buying This G350d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with evidence of regular oil changes using MB 229.5 or 229.51 spec oil
* DPF and AdBlue system regularly maintained (regular motorway runs)
* Camtronic system inspected (no popping intake noises)
avoidIf: * Persistent DPF warning lights with no resolution
* Popping noise from the intake (cam rocker wear warning)
* Loss of power or turbo lag (turbo or actuator issues)
* Poor service history — the OM656 is a sophisticated engine that demands good maintenance

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The G350d is powered by Mercedes' modern OM656 straight-six diesel — a sophisticated and refined engine that delivers exceptional performance and efficiency. It's the most powerful diesel G-Class ever, producing 286 hp and 600 Nm of torque. It also achieves the best fuel economy of any G-Class. The engine is generally reliable, but it has known weak points: DPF/AdBlue system issues (especially with city driving), Bosch CP4 fuel system sensitivity, turbocharger concerns, Camtronic valve timing issues, and a wiring harness recall. These are well-understood and manageable with proper maintenance. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained G350d is a £70,000–£100,000 luxury off-roader; a neglected one is a £7,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare G350d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes G350d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for popping noises from the intake (cam rocker wear warning)
* Service History — including the OM656 era (2019–present) and confirmed oil-change intervals using MB 229.5/229.51 spec oil
* DPF/AdBlue Check — Any DPF or AdBlue warning lights or regeneration issues? (city driving is high risk)
* Fuel System Check — Evidence of regular fuel filter changes and quality fuel use
* Turbo Check — Any loss of power, turbo lag, or unusual noises? (turbo or actuator warning)
* Wiring Harness Check — Has the wiring harness recall been completed? (potential chafing issue)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved DPF, fuel, turbo, or Camtronic issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
